aliases: Kampung chicken with skin and bone

english: Kampung chicken with skin and bone

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for boiled kampung (free-range) chicken with skin and bone; FOODON_03302050 (chicken food product) used as the closest defensible broad match.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (chicken meat, cooked) is the closest defensible match; no specific code exists for kampung (free-range) variety or boiling preparation with skin and bone retained.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226955001 is a broad substance-level match; no specific concept for boiled kampung chicken exists in SNOMED CT.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of boiled kampung chicken is approximately 150 g, representing a bone-in, skin-on portion such as a drumstick or thigh piece as commonly served in Singaporean and Malaysian households.

nutrient_source.title: Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food — HPB Singapore Foodo Food Composition Database, entry F11010002: Boiled kampung chicken (with skin and bone)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macronutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) taken directly from HPB Foodo entry F11010002 for boiled kampung chicken with skin and bone — exact match. Saturated fat (2.8 g), monounsaturated fat (3.9 g), polyunsaturated fat (2.2 g), potassium (220 mg), and cholesterol (88 mg) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for chicken, whole, meat and skin, stewed (NDB No. 05064), which is an approved secondary source; these values are approximate and documented in notes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index not applicable — boiled kampung chicken contains 0 g carbohydrate per 100 g; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Boiled kampung chicken contains 0 g carbohydrate per 100 g (HPB Foodo F11010002). Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. Per established GI methodology (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care), GI testing requires a minimum available carbohydrate load of 25 g; pure protein and fat foods are untestable and classified as not applicable. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 by definition. glycemic_index is set to null and gi_category to not_applicable per Veritas protocol for negligible-carbohydrate foods.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (42 mg/100 g) and moderate saturated fat (~2.8 g/100 g); no added salt in boiling preparation
hypertension.guidance: Boiled kampung chicken is suitable for individuals with hypertension due to its low sodium content of 42 mg/100 g; patients should remove the skin to reduce saturated fat and avoid high-sodium dipping sauces such as soy sauce or chilli sauce.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate (0 g/100 g) and zero sugar (0 g/100 g); high protein (25.7 g/100 g) supports satiety and glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Boiled kampung chicken is an excellent protein source for individuals with type 2 diabetes, as its zero carbohydrate content produces no postprandial glucose rise; pair with low-GI vegetables and avoid high-GI accompaniments such as large portions of white rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat approximately 2.8 g/100 g (well below 5 g/100 g threshold); low sodium; high lean protein supports cardiovascular health when skin is removed
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Boiled kampung chicken presents low cardiovascular risk when consumed without skin, as saturated fat is approximately 2.8 g/100 g and sodium is 42 mg/100 g; patients with cardiovascular disease should remove the skin before eating and limit portion size to 100–150 g per meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Boiling avoids AGE formation; saturated fat below RA threshold; however, no omega-3 or anti-inflammatory compounds present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Boiled kampung chicken is acceptable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis as boiling avoids advanced glycation end-product formation and saturated fat is below 5 g/100 g; however, it provides no omega-3 fatty acids or anti-inflammatory polyphenols, so it should be paired with anti-inflammatory foods such as oily fish, turmeric, or cruciferous vegetables.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Boiled kampung chicken (水煮甘榜鸡 / Ayam kampung rebus) is a traditional Singaporean and Malaysian dish made by simmering free-range kampung chicken — retained with skin and bone — in plain water or a lightly seasoned broth until fully cooked. Kampung chicken, meaning 'village chicken' in Malay, is a free-range breed prized for its firmer, leaner, and more flavourful meat compared to commercially farmed broiler chicken. The boiling method preserves the natural flavour of the bird while keeping sodium low (42 mg/100 g) and avoiding the formation of advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) associated with high-heat dry cooking methods such as frying or grilling. The resulting meat is tender yet slightly firmer than broiler chicken, with a clean, savoury flavour and a mildly gelatinous skin. Nutritionally, boiled kampung chicken is high in protein (25.7 g/100 g), moderate in fat (10.1 g/100 g, of which approximately 2.8 g is saturated fat), and contains zero carbohydrate and zero sugar, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate, ketogenic, and diabetic dietary patterns. Calcium (39 mg/100 g) and iron (0.8 mg/100 g) are present in modest amounts. It is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. Clinically, it is appropriate for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease, particularly when the skin is removed before consumption. It is commonly served as part of a rice meal, alongside clear soup, or as a protein component in hawker-centre and home-cooked meals across Singapore and Malaysia.
common_user_queries: Is kampung chicken good for diabetes?
Is boiled chicken good for high blood pressure?
Kampung chicken vs broiler chicken nutrition

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients and micronutrients sourced directly from HPB Foodo Singapore database (F11010002), which is the primary approved regional source for Singaporean food records. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and cholesterol estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (chicken, whole, meat and skin, stewed, NDB 05064) as the closest approved secondary source; these are documented in notes. GI is not applicable due to zero carbohydrate content, with methodology reference to Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care) as an approved peer-reviewed GI source.
notes: 1. ZERO-MACRO JUSTIFICATION: carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo F11010002 — boiled kampung chicken contains no carbohydrate-bearing ingredients. sugar_g is naturally zero as no sugars are present in plain boiled chicken. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as chicken meat contains no dietary fibre. 2. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES (-1): trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 because these values are not reported in the HPB Foodo F11010002 entry and detailed vitamin, mineral, and moisture data for this specific traditional preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore database as of 2026. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 05064, chicken whole, meat and skin, stewed) was consulted for partial fatty acid and mineral data. 3. SATURATED FAT ESTIMATION: saturated_fat_g (2.8 g/100 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (3.9 g/100 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (2.2 g/100 g), potassium_mg (220 mg/100 g), and cholesterol_mg (88 mg/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for chicken, whole, meat and skin, stewed (NDB 05064), as HPB Foodo does not separately report these values for F11010002. These are approximate and may differ from kampung chicken due to breed and feed differences. 4. GI NOT APPLICABLE: Boiled kampung chicken contains 0 g carbohydrate per 100 g; glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. glycemic_index is set to null and gi_category to not_applicable per Veritas protocol. GI methodology reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283 (University of Sydney GI Database / peer-reviewed journal). 5. ONTOLOGY: No FoodOn or FoodEx2 code exists specifically for boiled kampung (free-range) chicken with skin and bone; broad/adapted matches documented in ontology section with match_type set to closest_parent. 6. MULTILINGUAL NAMES: Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi names provided as this is a Singaporean dish per G6 multilingual compliance rules.
